transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Good morning everyone. First thing — how was the weekend? Any prod issues?

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: Clean. 3 actual transactions came through on Saturday. All 3 successful. The 2 intermittent cases we were worried about — neither of them triggered over the weekend.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: I was checking from my phone every few hours. No alerts fired. But I want to say clearly — those 2 intermittent cases are still open on the board. I don't want anyone closing them just because the weekend was quiet.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: Agreed. Don't close them. Label them monitor-pending and keep them visible. Good call Ajay.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: Infra side also clean. No connectivity issues, cert is holding fine.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Good. Okay now — there's a bigger conversation we need to have. Client sent an email Friday evening, after our go-live communication. They've dropped the Sprint 5 scope. Inventory sync module, returns flow, and admin dashboard. They want all three in Sprint 5.

transcript.speaker: Suraj
transcript.text: All three? Admin dashboard alone is minimum 3 weeks of frontend work. Have they seen how complex that is?

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: Returns flow also is not simple. It directly touches the payment API we just built. Refund reversal logic is completely different from payment forward. Different endpoints, different state machine, different error handling.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: Rupali — honestly, these 3 together is 5-6 weeks of work minimum with our current team. And we're coming off a brutal sprint. The team needs reasonable planning, not another death march.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: I know. I already pushed back on the email. I said we need a proper scoping session. But client's response was — and I'm reading it directly — 'The same team delivered the payment module in this sprint under pressure, we're confident they can handle this scope.'

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: That's... not a reasonable response. We delivered payment with known issues still open, working late nights, Ajay testing at 1am. That's not a benchmark for future sprints.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: I hear you. Give me actual numbers. Not feelings — numbers. What is the effort for each item?

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: Okay. Inventory sync — 8 working days. Returns flow — 10 days minimum, could be 12 if the payment API intermittent issues interfere. Admin dashboard — 15 to 18 days. And these are not fully parallel. Returns flow depends on inventory sync being done first.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: So we're looking at roughly 5 to 6 weeks sequential.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: Yes. With proper QA buffer. And Ajay will remind us — those 2 intermittent payment issues from last Friday are still open. If returns flow reuses that same payment code path, we could be carrying a known bug into the new sprint.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: Exactly. I've been thinking about that since Friday. We can't build returns on top of something that has unresolved intermittent failures.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: Okay. I'll set up a scope call with client this Wednesday. I'll attend personally. Rakesh — I need a proper effort breakdown document by tomorrow EOD. Not a rough estimate — a proper doc with assumptions, dependencies, risks. Something I can show them.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: I'll prepare it. Will share by tomorrow 6pm.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Also Vivek — for Sprint 5, I want you looped in from day one on planning. Last sprint the infra issues cost us 2 days. We can't repeat that.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: Finally. Yes, please. I'll flag any infra dependency upfront if I know about it in advance.
